item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article has been replaced with a cc-compliant alternative. Image was taken by N_Aepic_Fael, edited by me to include the black bar, and is licensed under CC BY-SA 3.0.

item.title: Straightening a thing or two up:
item.content:encoded: I know that the main cognitohazardous effect here is driving people to paranoia believing that others have these horrible diseases. My question however is does the book directly effect the reader psychosomatically? Like can reading about a certain disease cause them to exhibit the symptoms and/or die without actually having the disease?

item.title: Admin Post - Closed
item.content:encoded: DividedCube, this is not an open collaboration article. You don't get to just add experiments willy-nilly to anything you feel like. As this counts as a major unauthorized edit, I've reverted the changes. Please do not make unauthorized edits to articles or you will not remain a member of the wiki.

item.title: Too Unscientific
item.content:encoded:
It's good and all, but something staying my upvote.

Currently the actual effects of the SCP are well known. The parameters aren't clear, but the effects are known and clear: as the O5 said, hypochondria by proxy, you read it, you're not affected. But everyone around thinks you're not only sick, but possibly infectious, even if the disease isn't infectious (the main entry in the research is about cancer).

But the entry doesn't says it. The SCP simply jumps to the log and let the reader conclude its effects. I think in this case, it was simply lazy writing. There is no reason to not explain it further before the log.

item.title: Question
item.content:encoded: To add a bit to the lore of this, Does this encyclopedia contain anomalous disease info as well as normal stuff, or is the Foundation's data filtering prowess that ungodly rediculous

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Encyclopedia Dramatica: the SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … how have I not upvoted this yet?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Subtle, hilarious, and profoundly disturbing. I'd even say 'not for the faint-of-heart': the way researchers go crazy and vivisect D-Class… URGH.

IIRC, Jerome K. Jerome mentioned something like this in Three Men in A Boat: one character reads a medical encyclopedia and ends up suffering from every single disease listed, except something I cannot find anywhere, like 'Synovial fluid leak' (épanchement de synovie in French).
When I started reading the skip I thought it would be the same, but… no. Much better!

Have a nice upvote guaranteed harmless for your health!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding this

+1, very nice article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a fantastic entry. Really great storytelling and I love how subtle the actual SCP is. You're clued in from the start with the change from Keter to Safe but up until the very end you're not really sure why.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very nice; subtle; sneaks up on you. :)

One suggestion: Wouldn't the Foundation know exactly how many pages long the book is? Perhaps list a specific page count (either clear text or wholly/partially redacted), rather than "approximately 1500 pages."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like SCPs like this, that appears as something different. I was about to downvote for researchers doing more and more senseless Mengele-sque experiments. Except not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm, i wonder if (1) it has an index or table of contents, like a normal book would have (or if it's kinda just like a dictionary where you just have to kinda read through till you find what you're looking for) and (2, and this is purely for pure curiosity) what the scientific name for SCP-008 would be.

Also, would it still have its effects if read via, say, remote drone? That would be an excellant way to record what the book has to say!

this thing is quite disturbing. I wonder how many of these damned things exist
(shudders)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A delightful example of an SCP that may appear funny at the first glance but if you think about it some more is anything but. Well done sir-or-madam!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Amazing entry. I was going to hold my vote because, when I saw the title, I thought it was going to be lame, cliched and worthy of a downvote, but the excellent writing bumped it to a novote. I started to suspect the twist around the lung cancer entries, and my suspicions were confirmed in a spectacular manner and skyrocketed it to an upvote.
Fantastic work, Lasergoose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Another one I have to confess I did not get when reading it, while I precisely like SCP that are not what they seem to be at first glance.
Upvoted for those two reasons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. It's called "WebMD."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God, I see people like this all the time. Except, it is usually due to WebMD. Could there be an online version of this out there?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted for copious amounts of YES!!.

I thoroughly enjoyed this one, it's just really well written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I read it the first time I thought "oh interesting, a book that gives you a disease. I could see how that would cause problems."

And then I went back and read it a second time after reading the last paragraph. This is excellent. Upvoted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good point. I'll go do that right now. And thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this one. Going back to read it the second time was even better. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The twist in this one is amazing. One of the best SCPs I read in a while.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It wouldn't be the first, but it IS very well done.

One point: The front cover and spine feature the title "The Encyclopedia of Common Diseases." The publisher's page indicates the book was printed in 19██ by █████ Press. No other copies of a book by that title have been found, and no record of the publisher exists. Other copies are available from Amazon (heck, there's a deluxe edition too!) I'd suggest something like "No other copies of a book by that title from that publisher have been found" or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first I thought the tone was terrible. Then I realized it was supposed to be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, you may have missed the part where all the researchers went crazy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… that's the whole point (which you seem to have missed).

This SCP induces hypochondria by proxy. It's not a biohazard, it's a cognitohazard.

Its effect is not "it makes the D-class cough", its effect is "it makes the Foundation researchers crazy paranoid about contagion".

It's almost as if someone made this specifically to fuck with the Foundation…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
HEY EVERYONE! THIS GUY IS COUGHING! IT MUST BE AN SCP!

But that's the point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Subject had developed persistent cough, despite never reading SCP-1025, and was placed in observation for one week."

HEY EVERYONE! THIS GUY IS COUGHING! IT MUST BE AN SCP! LET'S ALSO MENTION SEVERAL DISEASE-RELATED SCPS FOR NO COHERENT REASON!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One very nice touch is the fact that this thing was published by a non-existent company. It definitely is an anomalous object, and not just Foundation staff going batshit bugfuck nuts.

It reminds me of SCP-270, in a way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, all. I should stress that when I brought this concept to chat, it was literally a book that gave you diseases. soullesshuman and book (sorry, I don't know who book is on the wiki) suggested this. I think my execution was okay, but the concept was all them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was going to downvote it until I got to the last paragraph as it LOOKED like another "Wait, lets try THIS!"

The last paragraph fixed all that. Well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Stick it in a box, lock it up, and for God's sake, try not to worry about it."

That line nailed it for me, especially considering how many things out there have implied (or written) reams of test logs just trying to figure out the exact limits of its properties.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very well written, and an intriguing concept. Upvoted, and I'd do it again if I could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice twist on the whole evil book thing, upvoted :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This, one thousand times, also, I had to read it twice but it in a good way, and now have no idea why I didn't pick up on it after the Lung Cancer entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted hard for being a shining example of a "thing what makes you go crazy" done so very right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sweet. I wonder if the researchers/agents were focused on one illness in particular which they thought they might be coming down with and hallucinated symptoms of, and that drove them mad, or whether they were just afraid of contracting any of the fatal diseases.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aaaaaaaaaand here comes the upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It gives you medical student syndrome.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, extra special thanks to Soulless and Book, who helped flesh the idea out beyond "An X that does Y to you."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You've got it right, mostly. But it isn't intended to be a -J. It would in fact cause a horrible panic left out on a table somewhere. It gradually convinces people that those around them are horribly infected with bad things, and may or may not affect what the delusions are at first based on what the perceived victim has read.
Basically it's a lot of misdirection, and the O-5s see no benefit to figuring out the exact parameters at the cost of driving a bunch of researchers insane, when simply locking it up and not interacting with it works just as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So wait, is the actual SCP a book that convinces people nearby that it's capable of starting an epidemic? Alternately, if it's supposed to be funny, should it be a -J?

I still like it, it's just not the way most SCPs are written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hahaha this SCP. It's funny and yet oddly disquieting. Upvoted. Silly idea, lovely execution.
